title: Unexpected AI boom contributed to RBA interest rate U-turn
full_text:
Reserve Bank of Australia governor Michele Bullock has declared artificial intelligence is supercharging the world economy and is one of the reasons the bank was forced to U-turn on increasing interest rates.
Bullock told a Senate committee on Thursday that extraordinary growth in AI investment ignited the global economy last year, just as the RBA was expecting it to slow amid uncertainty caused by US President Donald Trump’s tariffs.
“As it turned out, through the second half of 2025 – and [it] continues now – the AI boom in the United States [has] absolutely supercharged everything,” Bullock said.
“We were seeing activity slow. The world was seeing activity slow. All of a sudden, everything reversed.”
Data released this week showed investment in data centres was responsible for almost all of Australia’s economic growth in the first three months of the year. Growth in business investment in the quarter rivalled the peak of the mining boom in 2012, even as the economy slowed during the early fallout from the war in the Middle East and interest rate increases.
Bullock pushed back on the suggestion the central bank’s decisions to cut the cash rate three times last year – decisions that have been reversed by three consecutive rate rises this year – were premature, in part due to the surprisingly robust performance of the global economy.
“I think we were doing the right thing at the right time,” she said. “[The] view about the Australian economy, and the world economy in that period – when the trade tensions were going on – was very, very, poor.”
Bullock said the risks posed to the global economy at the start of 2025 by Trump’s tariffs also failed to materialise, and this supported AI-related trade.
“Many of our trading partners were expecting to be very badly impacted by the trade restrictions and the tariffs, [but] they weren’t impacted nearly as much by those. And in addition, they got a huge tailwind from AI. So that’s really helped us as well,” Bullock said.
Economic commentary released by the RBA in November last year showed that AI-related exports drove growth in some of Australia’s major trading partners in East Asia last year, despite expectations that the region’s economy would slow.
“Growth in East Asia in late 2025 was stronger than expected,” the commentary said.
“Net exports contributed significantly to stronger-than-expected East Asian GDP growth in the December quarter, driven by robust growth in global demand for AI-enabling products such as semiconductors,” said Bullock.
RBA assistant governor Sarah Hunter said the central bank had commenced researching the long-term impacts of AI on the Australian economy, including impacts on productivity and the labour market.
“Obviously, it is a relatively new piece of work for us to be starting to undertake, but it is something that we’re now actively researching,” Hunter said.
Commonwealth Bank economists previously estimated that AI use – in particular for data entry, record keeping, and accounting – could allow the economy to grow by as much as 3 per cent a year without fuelling inflation because it would increase the nation’s productivity. The RBA believes the economy’s current growth speed limit is around 2 per cent.
YXJ0aWNsZTo2MTNkZWQ5MS0yZmQzLTQ3NTEtYTAzNC1mMTUzMDQ1NGQ2NjE
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Lea Jurkovic
heading: Data Science and AI
